=== PERSPECTIVE: Growth & Scale Analyst ===
**Growth & Scale Analyst Briefing: Zepto's Quick Commerce Dark Store Network in India**

**MEMORANDUM**

**TO:** Executive Leadership
**FROM:** Growth & Scale Analyst
**DATE:** October 26, 2024
**SUBJECT:** Deconstruction of Zepto's Quick Commerce Business Model and Network in India

This briefing analyzes Zepto's quick commerce dark store network in India, focusing on its unit economics, cost structures, growth trajectory, and competitive positioning against market leader Blinkit.

**1. Zepto's Core Business Model and Strategic Orientation:**
Zepto operates an inventory-led dark store model, promising 10-minute deliveries of daily essentials, now expanding into non-grocery categories like cosmetics and electronics. Founded in 2021 by Aadit Palicha and Kaivalya Vohra, Zepto's success is predicated on tech-enabled efficiency, utilizing predictive algorithms for inventory stocking and optimized delivery routes from hyperlocal dark stores (IJRPR57035.pdf, 42signals.com). Its youthful branding primarily targets Generation Z in high-density Tier 1 cities (Bussiness-Standard-case_study_Kanika-Malik.pdf).

**2. Unit Economics and Cost Structures:**
Quick commerce is inherently a high-cost, capital-intensive model.

*   **Dark Store Network & Costs:**
    *   Zepto leverages dark stores within a 1.5-2 km radius of delivery clusters (Bussiness-Standard-case_study_Kanika-Malik.pdf). These typically range from 2,500-4,000 sq ft, requiring upfront capex of INR 6-7 million (Quick Commerce Industry.pdf).
    *   High urban rentals are a significant "dark store drain," with aggressive competition driving up rents by 25-40% in contested micro-markets (base.com). Illustrative operating costs for a 3,500 sq ft dark store can be ₹792,500/month, with staff costs (₹425,000 for 25 staff) and rent (₹227,500) being major components (PPFAS Quick Commerce FOF). Fulfillment costs per order are estimated at ₹18.87 (PPFAS Quick Commerce FOF).
    *   Zepto aims to double its dark stores to 700 by March 2025 (NLZepto-6909ad5b-24ba-4a78-8e15-45f16400664f.pdf, livemint.com - $665-mn pre-IPO). Notably, 75% of its 350 dark stores are already EBITDA positive, with profitability achieved in 6 months, down from nearly two years, and targeting 3-4 months (livemint.com - $665-mn pre-IPO).

*   **Labor Costs:**
    *   The model relies heavily on a gig economy workforce for last-mile delivery. Delivery costs remain high due to urban traffic, complex addresses, and the necessity of frequent, small trips to maintain speed (base.com). Delivery partners in India typically earn around 10% of the order value, or ~$1.50 per order (linkedin.com - Arin Verma, medium.com - Speed vs Scale).

*   **Average Order Value (AOV) & Revenue Diversification:**
    *   Quick commerce orders are typically low AOV (₹300-₹400), challenging logistics profitability (Bussiness-Standard-case_study_Kanika-Malik.pdf, Quick Commerce In India_R1.pdf). A target AOV of ₹600-₹700 is needed for dark stores to cover costs (base.com).
    *   Zepto is actively increasing AOV through private labels, bundled discounts, curated combos, and expanding into higher-margin non-grocery categories like electronics and cosmetics (Bussiness-Standard-case_study_Kanika-Malik.pdf, NLZepto-6909ad5b-24ba-4a78-8e15-45f16400664f.pdf). Its "SuperSaver" feature aims to achieve 3x the AOV of regular deliveries (livemint.com - Mint Explainer: Why quick commerce wants planned buys).
    *   Revenue streams include commissions, delivery fees, and increasingly, advertising. Zepto's ad revenue hit ₹400 crore in May 2024, projected to reach ₹1,000 crore in 12 months, growing at 150% YoY (livemint.com - Zepto's ad revenue, loyalty programme).
    *   Zepto Pass, a subscription service launched in February 2024, has garnered 4.5 million subscribers and boasts a positive contribution margin due to increased order frequency and volume (livemint.com - Zepto's ad revenue, loyalty programme).

**3. Growth vs. Burn and Scalability Potential:**
India's quick commerce market grew from ~$1.5 billion in 2022 to ~$6-7 billion in 2024 and is projected to reach $40-50 billion by 2029 (MMA Global Playbook). The Total Addressable Market (TAM) is estimated at $45 billion, with current penetration at only 7% (Quick Commerce Industry.pdf).

*   **Rapid Growth & Improving Unit Economics**: Zepto's FY24 operating revenue doubled to ₹4,454 crore (livemint.com - Zepto FY24 revenue doubles). Despite increased expenses, its net loss remained flat at ₹1,248 crore in FY24 (livemint.com - Zepto FY24 revenue doubles), indicating improved operational efficiency and a path to profitability. The company is now "nearly EBITDA positive" at a company level (livemint.com - $665-mn pre-IPO).
*   **Capital Access**: Zepto has raised significant capital, with its valuation rapidly increasing from $570M (Dec 2021) to $3.6B (Jun 2024) and $5B (Aug 2024) (livemint.com - Zepto bags year’s highest fundraise, Quick Commerce Industry.pdf). This demonstrates strong investor confidence in its execution and market potential.
*   **Customer Acquisition & Retention**: Zepto reported adding 100,000 new customers weekly (Dec 2021) and boasts 95% repeat buyers (livemint.com - Quick commerce takes the fast lane). Subscription models like Zepto Pass further enhance retention (livemint.com - Zepto rolls out subscription service).

**4. Comparison with Blinkit:**
Blinkit (Zomato-backed) leads the quick commerce market with a 40% share, followed by Zepto at 28% (HSBC Global Research, livemint.com - Zepto FY24 revenue doubles).

*   **Network Size**: Blinkit had 1,007 dark stores as of Q3 FY25, targeting 2,000 by Dec 2025. Zepto had 700-750 by Nov 2024, aiming for 1,000 by Mar 2025 (Blinkit vs Zepto Product TearDown - NextLeap).
*   **Profitability**: Blinkit turned profitable in Q1 FY25 (Blinkit vs Zepto Product TearDown - NextLeap), slightly ahead of Zepto's current "nearly EBITDA positive" status.
*   **Operating Model**: Blinkit operates a marketplace model (livemint.com - Zepto to file IPO draft papers), while Zepto, traditionally inventory-led, is transitioning to a marketplace model in preparation for its IPO (livemint.com - Zepto to file IPO draft papers).
*   **Growth**: Both demonstrate strong growth; Blinkit's revenue surged 2.4X YoY in Q1 FY25 (Blinkit vs Zepto Product TearDown - NextLeap), while Zepto's operating revenue doubled in FY24 (livemint.com - Zepto FY24 revenue doubles).

**5. Positive Growth Indicators & UPI Rails:**
The rapid expansion of quick commerce is heavily reliant on India's robust digital infrastructure. The "rise of smartphones, digital payments, and app-based shopping" has been crucial (IJRPR57035.pdf). Widespread digital payments, particularly UPI rails, facilitate instant and frequent transactions, directly contributing to high transaction velocity and enabling impulse purchases. This ease of payment reduces friction, supporting customer acquisition and repeat orders.

**6. Estimated Diffusion Parameters and Concentration:**
*   **Bass Diffusion Parameters**:
    *   **Innovation Coefficient (p)**: High. The quick adoption during and post-pandemic, driven by the novelty of 10-minute deliveries and convenience, suggests a strong initial innovator uptake. The rapid entry of players and immediate consumer shift highlight a high 'p'.
    *   **Imitation Coefficient (q)**: High. The intense competition, rapid scaling of dark store networks by multiple players, and swift replication of successful strategies (e.g., subscription passes, non-grocery expansion) indicate significant social contagion and imitation amongst potential adopters.
*   **Power Law Alpha Concentration Exponent**:
    *   The market is highly concentrated, with Blinkit (40%) and Zepto (28%) dominating a significant majority of the market share, followed by Swiggy Instamart. This suggests a **high Alpha value** (likely >1.5-2.0), indicating a "winner-take-most" or oligopolistic structure where a few large players capture a disproportionate share of the market, driven by network effects and economies of scale.

**Conclusion:**
Zepto demonstrates strong growth and improving unit economics, driven by operational efficiency, strategic diversification, and effective customer engagement through initiatives like Zepto Pass and a burgeoning advertising revenue stream. The sector's inherent high-burn model is being mitigated by scaled operations and market dominance. While direct numerical Bass Diffusion parameters and Power Law Alpha are not explicitly calculable from the corpus, observed trends point to high innovation and imitation coefficients, and a concentrated market structure. Continued focus on cost optimization, AOV expansion, and disciplined dark store expansion will be critical for Zepto's long-term sustainability and projected IPO.


=== PERSPECTIVE: Unit Economics Auditor ===
**MEMORANDUM**

**TO:** Interested Stakeholders
**FROM:** Unit Economics Auditor (Bear Case)
**DATE:** October 26, 2024
**SUBJECT:** Deconstruction of Zepto's Quick Commerce Dark Store Network in India: Unit Economics, Costs, and Growth vs. Burn Analysis

This briefing deconstructs the unit economics and operational model of Zepto’s quick commerce dark store network in India, specifically highlighting critical cost drivers, profitability challenges, and competitive dynamics through a bear-case lens.

**Zepto's Core Business Model and Operating Structure**
Zepto operates an inventory-led dark store model focusing on 10-minute delivery of daily essentials in metropolitan areas (IJRPR57035.pdf, markhub24.com). Its strategy prioritizes operational efficiency, leveraging technology for demand forecasting, real-time inventory management, and route optimization (ijrpr.com, grm.institute, ijcr.org, 42signals.com). Dark stores are strategically placed within 1.5-2 km delivery clusters (grm.institute, ijcr.org). Revenue is generated through commissions, delivery fees, advertising, and subscription services like "Zepto Pass" (42signals.com, livemint.com - 11718950801678, livemint.com - 11709192210962).

**Unit Economics Under Scrutiny**

1.  **Average Order Value (AOV) & Basket Size**: Zepto struggles with typically low AOVs, ranging from ₹300-600 per order, driven by impulse purchases (grm.institute, livemint.com - 11718536515304). This makes covering fixed logistics costs challenging on a per-transaction basis. While initiatives like "SuperSaver" aim to increase AOV to ₹600-700 (livemint.com - 11770014400091, base.com), analysts caution that such growth is often "bought with deeper discounts," which can "squeeze Ebitda margins" (livemint.com - 11770014400091).

2.  **Dark Store Costs**:
    *   **Rent**: A significant fixed cost, exacerbated by aggressive expansion in prime urban locations. Competition for these spaces has reportedly driven up rents by "25-40% in contested micro-markets" (base.com). Dark stores are typically leased properties (chryseum.in).
    *   **Staffing & Fulfillment**: High operating costs are a common constraint across quick commerce firms (ijrpr.com, grm.institute). Illustrative unit economics for a generic dark store show fulfillment costs at approximately ₹18.87 per order (PPFAS - Quick Commerce FOF). Zepto's "Packman" WMS aims to optimize picking efficiency to reduce in-store labor time (42signals.com).
    *   **Inventory Leakage**: While Zepto focuses on high-demand items to avoid unsold inventory loss (ijcr.org), perishable goods still face a spoilage rate of "5-15%" depending on location and season (medium.com).

3.  **Delivery Costs**: Last-mile delivery remains the "largest variable expense" (base.com). Riders are typically paid per delivery, averaging approximately $1.50 per order (medium.com), with Indian delivery partners earning around "10% of the order value per delivery" (linkedin.com - arinverma). This cost does not reduce significantly with scale, as ultra-fast delivery demands low batching rates (medium.com).

4.  **Gross and Contribution Margins**: The sector is burdened with "thin margins" and often "negative contribution margins" per order (grm.institute, journals.sagepub.com). While Zepto Pass has a positive contribution margin, it relies on "higher order volumes and frequency" (livemint.com - 11718950801678).

**Growth Trajectory vs. Cash Burn**
Zepto is identified as operating in a "high-burn" and "cash-guzzling" category (grm.institute, livemint.com - 11692948558669). In FY24, Zepto's operating revenue doubled to ₹4,454 crore, yet its net loss remained flat at ₹1,248 crore, on total expenses of ₹5,747 crore (livemint.com - 11734105378878). This suggests that revenue growth is largely offset by escalating operational costs, challenging the narrative of improved efficiency.

Zepto has aggressively expanded its dark store network, planning to double its current ~350 stores to 700 by March 2025 (nextleap.app, chryseum.in, livemint.com - 11718939449690). While Zepto claims 75% of its dark stores are now EBITDA positive and the time to store profitability has shrunk from two years to six months (livemint.com - 11718950801678), the overall company-level EBITDA positivity is still a future target (livemint.com - 11692948558669).

**Comparison with Blinkit**
Blinkit (Zomato-owned) holds a larger market share at 40% compared to Zepto's 28% (HSBC report, livemint.com - 11734105378878). Blinkit also boasts a larger dark store footprint, with 1,007 stores as of Q3 FY25, targeting 2,000 by December 2025 (NextLeap - Blinkit vs Zepto Product TearDown, chryseum.in). Blinkit demonstrated Q1 FY25 revenue of ₹942 crore and notably "turned profitable with a profit of ₹43 crore" in the same quarter (NextLeap - Blinkit vs Zepto Product TearDown). Blinkit's strategy emphasizes "operational efficiencies" and avoids "deep discounting on bulk buys" for sustainable growth, a contrast to Zepto's AOV-boosting tactics (livemint.com - 11770014400091).

**Bear Case Risks and Outlook**
The quick commerce sector faces a "Prisoner's Dilemma" (linkedin.com - arinverma), where intense competition drives discount wars and high Customer Acquisition Costs (CAC), leading to capital dependence (base.com, grm.institute, livemint.com - 11718939449690). Regulatory risks around gig worker rights and zoning laws also persist (grm.institute, livemint.com - 11775118505267). Zepto's planned transition to a marketplace model (livemint.com - 11735963052646) introduces new operational complexities and potential quality control challenges. While Zepto is pursuing an IPO, its ability to sustain profitability at scale, particularly as it expands into higher-value non-grocery items, remains to be proven without significant margin erosion from competitive pressures and increased delivery complexity.


=== PERSPECTIVE: Macro & Sovereign Strategist ===
**Macro & Sovereign Strategist Briefing: Zepto's Quick Commerce Dark Store Network in India**

**Date:** October 26, 2024
**Subject:** Deconstruction of Zepto's Quick Commerce Dark Store Business Model and Comparison with Blinkit

**1. Executive Summary**
Zepto, a prominent quick commerce player in India, operates an inventory-led dark store model focusing on ultra-fast delivery of daily essentials. Its business model, while demonstrating rapid growth and significant investor confidence, is characterized by high operational costs, low average order values (AOV), and a substantial cash burn. Zepto is actively pursuing operational efficiencies and revenue diversification (subscriptions, advertising) to achieve profitability. In comparison, Blinkit, the market leader, has already achieved company-level profitability in Q1 FY25, indicating differing stages of financial maturity and strategic execution.

**2. Zepto's Dark Store Network and Operations**
Zepto, founded in 2021, pioneered a 10-minute grocery delivery model using hyperlocal "dark stores" (micro-fulfillment centers) not accessible to the public. These stores are strategically located within 1.5-2 km of delivery clusters in densely populated urban areas, maximizing order density and minimizing last-mile delivery times (IJRPR, GRM Institute, IJCRT).

*   **Scale**: Zepto operated approximately 350 dark stores in FY24, with plans to double this to 700 by March 2025, and aiming for 1,000 stores by March 2025 (NextLeap, Chryseum, Livemint Jun 2024, Livemint Dec 2024, Livemint Jun 2024 Funding).
*   **Store Characteristics**: Typically 2,500-4,000 sq ft, these stores stock high-demand items (2,500+ SKUs) to avoid unsold inventory (IJCRT, Chryseum). Zepto is expanding to bigger dark stores to stock a larger assortment, including non-grocery high-value items like electronics, mirroring competitors (Livemint Jun 2024).
*   **Technology Integration**: Advanced algorithms, AI-powered demand forecasting, real-time inventory management, and optimized routing are critical. Zepto uses "Zepto Packman" for efficient picking within dark stores (<60 seconds per order) (IJCRT, 42signals).

**3. Unit Economics and Costs**

Quick commerce is an inherently high-cost model, burdened by thin margins and complex logistics (GRM Institute, Base Blog).

*   **Revenue Streams**: Zepto generates revenue through commissions (take rate of 22-23%), delivery fees, and increasingly, advertising (Livemint Jun 2024 Ad, NextLeap, 42signals). The "Zepto Pass" subscription program, offering unlimited free deliveries and discounts for ₹19-₹39/month, is a significant growth driver, achieving 4.5 million subscribers and a positive contribution margin (Livemint Feb 2024, Livemint Jun 2024 Ad).
*   **Store Rent (Illustrative)**: A significant fixed cost. For an illustrative dark store (3,500 sq ft at ₹65/sq ft), monthly rent is approximately ₹227,500 (PPFAS FOF). Intense competition for urban spaces has driven up rents by 25-40% in contested micro-markets (Base Blog). Upfront capex for a new dark store is ₹6-7 million (Chryseum).
*   **Labor Costs (Illustrative)**: A dark store with 25 staff (3 shifts) has a total staff cost of approximately ₹425,000 per month (PPFAS FOF).
*   **Delivery Costs**: Last-mile delivery remains the largest variable expense (Base Blog). Riders are typically paid per delivery (around $1.50 per order or ~10% of order value in India) (Medium, LinkedIn Arin Verma). High urban traffic and complex addresses contribute to this cost (Base Blog).
*   **Average Order Value (AOV) Challenge**: Quick commerce thrives on impulse purchases, leading to low AOVs (typically ₹300-₹400 for Zepto) making logistics costs difficult to cover per transaction (GRM Institute, Livemint Feb 2026, Base Blog). Zepto aims for an AOV of ₹600-₹700 for profitability (Base Blog). Initiatives like "SuperSaver" are designed to increase AOV by encouraging bulk purchases, with a target of 3x AOV (Livemint Feb 2026).
*   **Spoilage**: Perishable goods lead to a spoilage rate of 5-15% (Medium).
*   **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)**: High due to fierce competition and heavy discounting to attract users (GRM Institute, Base Blog).

**4. Growth vs. Burn and Profitability Pathway**

Zepto operates in a "high-growth yet high-burn category" (GRM Institute), necessitating continuous fundraising to fuel expansion and achieve sustainability.

*   **Funding & Valuation**: Raised over $600 million within two years, achieving unicorn status ($1.4 billion in August 2023) and recently raising $665 million in a pre-IPO round at a $3.6 billion valuation in June 2024 (GRM Institute, Livemint Aug 2023, Livemint Jun 2024 Funding).
*   **Revenue Growth**: Zepto's operating revenue more than doubled year-on-year to ₹4,454 crore in FY24 (Livemint Dec 2024), up from ₹2,024 crore in FY23 (Livemint Jun 2024). It is nearing $3 billion in annualized sales (Livemint Dec 2024).
*   **Losses**: Losses remained relatively flat at ₹1,248 crore in FY24, compared to ₹1,272 crore in FY23 (Livemint Dec 2024).
*   **Profitability Drivers**: Zepto is focused on a "frugality" culture and operational efficiency (Livemint Aug 2023). Key strategies include:
    *   Increasing AOV through category expansion (cosmetics, gifts, toys) and features like SuperSaver (Livemint Feb 2026, Livemint Jun 2024 Ad).
    *   Launching private labels with 20-30% better margins (GRM Institute).
    *   Enhancing customer loyalty with subscription plans like Zepto Pass, which also increases order frequency and volume (Livemint Feb 2024, Livemint Jun 2024 Ad).
    *   Improving forecasting to minimize wastage (Livemint Jun 2024 Ad).
*   **EBITDA Positivity**: Zepto claims 75% of its 350 dark stores are EBITDA positive, with the time to achieve profitability per store reduced from nearly two years to six months, aiming for 3-4 months (Livemint Jun 2024 Funding, Livemint Jun 2024 Ad). The company states it is "nearly EBITDA positive" at a company level (Livemint Jun 2024 Funding) and on track to be near PAT (net profit) positive in two quarters (Livemint Feb 2024).
*   **IPO Plans**: Zepto is preparing for a multi-billion dollar public market listing in FY25-26, transitioning its operating model to a marketplace structure and moving its base to India from Singapore (Livemint Feb 2024, Livemint Jan 2025 IPO).

**5. Comparison with Blinkit**

*   **Market Share**: Blinkit (Zomato-owned) holds the largest market share at 40%, while Zepto has steadily increased its share to 28% (HSBC Global Research, April 2024) (Chryseum, Livemint Dec 2024).
*   **Strategic Orientation**: Blinkit emphasizes speed and dense network expansion (IJRPR), offers a comprehensive product range (~7,000 products), and focuses on robust supply chain and strong supplier relationships, primarily in North Indian cities (Chryseum, NextLeap). Zepto prioritizes operational efficiency and initial urban focus (IJRPR, GRM Institute).
*   **Dark Store Footprint**: Blinkit has a larger network with 1,007 dark stores (Q3 FY25), targeting 2,000 by December 2025 (NextLeap, Chryseum). Zepto's plans aim for 700-1,000 stores by March 2025 (NextLeap, Livemint Jun 2024 Funding).
*   **Profitability**: **Blinkit has achieved profitability**, reporting a profit of ₹43 crore in Q1 FY25, with revenue surging 2.4x YoY to ₹942 crore (NextLeap). In contrast, Zepto's losses remained flat in FY24, though it is "nearly EBITDA positive" (Livemint Dec 2024, Livemint Jun 2024 Funding). This indicates Blinkit has demonstrated a quicker path to overall company-level profitability.
*   **Business Model**: Blinkit already operates on a marketplace model (Livemint Jan 2025 IPO, businessmodelcanvastemplate.com). Zepto is currently transitioning from an inventory-led (holding stock on books) to a marketplace model in preparation for its IPO (Livemint Jan 2025 IPO, Livemint Dec 2024).
*   **Valuation**: Blinkit's implied value, driven by its improved performance and profitability, was $13 billion in March 2024 (Livemint Jun 2024 Funding), significantly higher than Zepto's $3.6 billion valuation (Livemint Jun 2024 Funding).

In conclusion, Zepto's quick commerce model in India is a high-growth, capital-intensive venture heavily reliant on operational efficiency and customer retention to navigate thin margins. While demonstrating strong execution and investor backing, its path to overall profitability remains in progress, with market leader Blinkit currently having a stronger financial track record in terms of profitability. The sector remains dynamic, with fierce competition, requiring continuous innovation in unit economics, revenue diversification, and service expansion.


=== PERSPECTIVE: Global Precedents Researcher ===
**Briefing Memo: Deconstructing Zepto's Quick Commerce Dark Store Network in India**

**To:** Global Precedents Research Team
**From:** Global Precedents Researcher
**Date:** October 26, 2024
**Subject:** Comparative Analysis: Zepto's Quick Commerce Business Model and Unit Economics in India vs. Blinkit

This briefing deconstructs Zepto's quick commerce dark store network in India, analyzing its unit economics, store rent, labor costs, growth vs. burn, and offering a comparative perspective against its peer, Blinkit. The quick commerce sector in India has emerged as a high-growth, high-burn category, necessitating robust operational efficiency and diversified revenue streams to achieve sustainability.

**1. Zepto's Business Model and Dark Store Network:**
Zepto, founded in 2021 by Aadit Palicha and Kaivalya Vohra, pioneered a 10-minute grocery delivery model in India. Its core strategy relies on an **inventory-led dark store model** (IJRPR57035.pdf, 42signals.com), strategically placed within 1.5-2 km delivery clusters in high-density Tier 1 cities (grm.institute, Zepto Business Model - How This Changed Quick Commerce). As of FY24, Zepto operated 350 dark stores (NLZepto-6909ad5b-24ba-4a78-8e15-45f16400664f), with plans to double this to 700 by March 2025 and aim for 1,000 stores (Quick Commerce Industry, Zepto bags year’s highest fundraise, Zepto’s FY24 revenue doubles). Dark stores are typically 2500-4000 sq. ft., leased properties, with an upfront capex of INR 6-7 million per store (Quick Commerce Industry). They stock ~2,500 to 45,000 items, focusing on high-demand goods to minimize unsold inventory (IJCRT25A4378.pdf, Zepto’s Unit Economics).

Technology is central to Zepto's efficiency, utilizing AI for demand forecasting, real-time inventory management, route planning (IJCRT25A4378.pdf), and a proprietary warehouse management system called "Zepto Packman" for optimized picking routes (42signals.com).

**2. Unit Economics - Challenges:**
The quick commerce model is inherently capital-intensive and faces significant challenges:
*   **High Operational Costs**: Burdened by thin margins and complex logistics (grm.institute).
*   **High Urban Rentals**: Dark stores require prime urban locations to meet 10-20 minute delivery promises, leading to a 25-40% increase in rents in contested micro-markets (base.com). Fixed costs for a dark store (rent, labor, utilities, refrigeration) can be approximately $20,000 per month (medium.com).
*   **Low Average Order Value (AOV)**: Q-commerce orders are typically for immediate needs, resulting in low AOVs (₹300-400) (grm.institute, base.com). To cover fixed and variable costs, platforms aim for AOVs of ₹600-₹700 (base.com). This often results in a negative unit-level profit per order (e.g., -$0.75 loss per $11 order, excluding CAC and tech costs) (medium.com).
*   **Labor Costs**: Highly dependent on a gig economy workforce (grm.institute). Delivery costs remain high due to urban traffic, complex addresses, and frequent small trips, averaging $1.50 per order (medium.com). India's rider payouts are about 10% of order value, compared to 20% in the US, straining margins (linkedin.com/posts/arinverma). Dark store staff costs also contribute significantly to operating expenses (Quick Commerce FOF).
*   **Inventory Management**: Focus on perishable goods leads to spoilage rates of 5-15% (medium.com).
*   **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)**: High due to intense competition and heavy discounting to attract users (grm.institute, base.com).
*   **Regulatory Risks**: Concerns around zoning laws, labor rights, and carbon emissions from frequent micro-deliveries (grm.institute, livemint.com/start-ups/food-delivery-swiggy).

**3. Unit Economics - Revenue & Profitability Levers:**
Zepto is actively pursuing strategies to improve unit economics and profitability:
*   **Diversified Revenue Streams**:
    *   **Commissions & Delivery Fees**: Primary revenue sources (42signals.com).
    *   **Advertising Revenue**: A significant growth driver, reaching ₹400 crore in May 2024 and projected to hit ₹1,000 crore in the next 12 months, growing at 150% YoY. This drives margins steadily (Zepto's ad revenue, Advertising brings a new revenue stream).
    *   **Subscription Plans (Zepto Pass)**: Launched in Feb 2024, priced at ₹19-₹39/month, offering free deliveries and discounts. It has gained 4.5 million subscribers, increasing user loyalty, order frequency, and contributing positively to margins (Zepto rolls out subscription, Zepto's ad revenue, Zepto bags year’s highest fundraise).
    *   **Private Labels**: Launching own-branded essentials with 20-30% better margins (grm.institute).
*   **Increasing AOV/GOV**: Efforts include bundled discounts, curated combos, and features like "SuperSaver" (testing discounts on orders >₹1,000, potential for 3x AOV) (Mint Explainer: Why quick commerce wants planned buys, grm.institute). Expanding into non-grocery categories like electronics, appliances, and cosmetics also boosts AOV (Zepto plans bigger dark stores, Zepto bags year’s highest fundraise).
*   **Operational Efficiency**: Focus on data-driven dark store location, real-time inventory management, optimized routing, and improved forecasting to minimize wastage (IJCRT25A4378.pdf, Zepto's ad revenue). Zepto claims 75% of its 350 dark stores are EBITDA-positive, taking 6 months to become profitable (down from 23 months), with an aim to reduce this to 3-4 months (Zepto bags year’s highest fundraise, Zepto's ad revenue).

**4. Growth vs. Burn:**
Zepto has demonstrated significant growth while actively managing its cash burn:
*   **Funding & Valuation**: Raised over $600 million, achieving unicorn status at $1.4 billion in August 2023. A subsequent $665 million pre-IPO round in June 2024 doubled its valuation to $3.6 billion (Zepto raises $200 million, Zepto bags year’s highest fundraise).
*   **Revenue Growth**: Operating revenue more than doubled from ₹142 crore in FY22 to ₹2,024 crore in FY23, and further to ₹4,454 crore in FY24 (Zepto’s FY24 revenue doubles, Zepto plans bigger dark stores).
*   **Losses**: While total expenses increased by nearly 65% in FY24, losses remained flat at ₹1,248 crore in FY24, compared to ₹1,272 crore in FY23 (Zepto’s FY24 revenue doubles). Zepto claims to have reduced its burn rate 5X on a per-order basis (Zepto nears unicorn status).
*   **Profitability Outlook**: The company is nearing EBITDA positivity at a company level and is on track for a public listing in FY25-26, having filed a confidential DRHP (Zepto rolls out subscription, Zepto to file IPO draft papers, Zepto raises $200 million, Zepto bags year’s highest fundraise).

**5. Comparison with Blinkit:**
Blinkit (Zomato-owned) and Zepto are the leading players in India's quick commerce market, sharing common challenges but with differing strategic emphases:
*   **Strategic Orientation**: Blinkit emphasizes speed and dense network expansion, while Zepto prioritizes operational efficiency (IJRPR57035.pdf).
*   **Market Share**: Blinkit holds a larger share at 40%, compared to Zepto's 28% (HSBC Global Research, April report cited in Zepto’s FY24 revenue doubles).

## BRIEFING MEMORANDUM: Deconstructing Zepto's Quick Commerce Dark Store Network in India – A Labor & Human Capital Perspective

**TO:** Interested Stakeholders in Labor & Human Capital
**FROM:** Labor & Human Capital Advocate
**DATE:** October 26, 2024
**SUBJECT:** Analysis of Zepto's Quick Commerce Model: Unit Economics, Labor Costs, Growth, and Comparison with Blinkit

This briefing deconstructs the business model of Zepto’s quick commerce dark store network in India, analyzing its unit economics, significant cost drivers, growth trajectory, and comparing it against key competitor Blinkit. Our lens primarily focuses on the inherent human capital pressures and social outcomes within this high-speed, high-burn industry.

### Zepto's Quick Commerce Business Model Overview

Zepto, founded in 2021, operates an inventory-led quick commerce model delivering groceries and essentials within 10 minutes through a network of hyperlocal "dark stores" (IJCRT25A4378.pdf, 42signals.com). Its competitive advantage is built on delivery speed, tech-enabled efficiency (e.g., Zepto Packman WMS, AI for route optimization and inventory), and urban density focus (grm.institute). Zepto is strategically expanding its product categories beyond groceries into higher-value items like electronics and apparel to boost average order values (AOVs) and improve margins (markhub24.com, livemint.com - 1718536515304).

### Unit Economics and Operational Costs: The Human Cost Equation

Quick commerce is an inherently expensive model, characterized by high fixed costs and relentless capital expenditure (base.com).

1.  **Average Order Value (AOV):** Quick commerce thrives on impulse buys, leading to low AOVs, typically ₹300-₹400 for Zepto (grm.institute). To cover fixed and variable costs, platforms need to push AOV to ₹600-₹700 (base.com). Zepto is attempting to increase this through features like "SuperSaver" and "Zepto Pass" subscriptions (livemint.com - 11770014400091, livemint.com - 11709192210962). However, analysts warn that this growth is often "bought with deeper discounts, free-delivery thresholds, and incentives that weigh on margins" (livemint.com - 11770014400091).

2.  **Dark Store Infrastructure:** Zepto operates hundreds of dark stores (250 as of June 2024, aiming for 700 by March 2025) (livemint.com - 1718536515304, livemint.com - 11734105378878). These micro-fulfillment centers, typically 2500-4000 sq. ft., are located in densely populated urban areas within a 2-3km radius of customers (ijrpr.com - V6ISSUE12, chryseum.in). This proximity drives "prime urban rent," with aggressive expansion causing rents to surge by **25-40%** in contested micro-markets (base.com). Illustrative unit economics suggest a dark store’s total staff cost can be **₹425,000 per month** (53.63% of total operating costs for 25 staff at ₹17,000/month average salary) (ppfasfof.com).

3.  **Labor Costs and Human Capital Pressure (Risks & Bottlenecks):**
    *   **Delivery Costs:** The model is "entirely dependent on a massive, flexible fleet of riders" (base.com). Delivery costs remain high, averaging an estimated **$1.50 per order** in quick commerce (medium.com) or **₹45.00-₹46.64 per order** for Blinkit (ppfasfof.com). In India, delivery partners earn roughly **10% of the order value** per delivery, compared to ~20% in the US (linkedin.com - arinverma).
    *   **Gig Worker Dependence & Exploitation:** The reliance on gig workers presents significant challenges concerning "regulation, reliability, demand, and attrition" (grm.institute). Zepto states its delivery personnel doing full-day shifts receive a "minimum wage of ₹20,000 and can earn more if they deliver more," with some completing up to 12 deliveries daily (livemint.com - 111662052923972).
    *   **Safety & Pressure:** The "10-minute delivery" promise has raised "concerns around labor rights of delivery partners, and the carbon emissions" (grm.institute). The Indian government has urged companies to drop these claims due to "rising safety risks for gig workers," following a "nationwide strike in December, when thousands of delivery riders protested low pay, constant pressure, and dangerous road conditions" (livemint.com - 1175118505267). Critically, "delivery costs are reported... under 'other expenses' instead of being treated as a core operating cost," which "makes the human cost of the last mile easy to ignore" (linkedin.com - arinverma). This highlights a policy bottleneck in transparent cost reporting and ethical labor practices.
    *   **Picking Costs:** While not specifically stated for Zepto, picking costs in emerging markets average **2-3% of revenue** (20vc.com), which directly impacts dark store staff efficiency.

4.  **Inventory Management:** Dark stores prioritize high-demand SKUs to minimize spoilage (ijcrt.org). However, perishable goods still incur a spoilage rate of **5-15%** (medium.com), adding to costs.

### Growth Trajectory vs. Financial Burn

Zepto has seen rapid growth, with operating revenue more than doubling year-on-year to **₹4,454 crore in FY24**, up from ₹142 crore in FY22 (livemint.com - 11734105378878). It secured significant funding, becoming India’s first unicorn of 2023 with a **$1.4 billion valuation** (livemint.com - 11692948558669) and later raised **$665 million at a $3.6 billion valuation** (livemint.com - 11718939449690).

Despite this growth and funding, Zepto's losses remained relatively flat at **₹1,248 crore in FY24** (vs. ₹1,272 crore in FY23) (livemint.com - 11734105378878), indicating a continued "high burn model" (grm.institute). Zepto claims 75% of its 350 dark stores are EBITDA-positive, with time to profitability shrinking to six months (from two years), aiming for 3-4 months (livemint.com - 11718939449690, livemint.com - 11718950801678). This focuses on operational maturity to achieve company-level EBITDA positivity.

### Comparative Analysis with Blinkit

Blinkit, a Zomato-owned entity, is the market leader with a **40% share** compared to Zepto's **28%** (HSBC via livemint.com - 11734105378878). Blinkit had **1,007 dark stores** as of Q3 FY25, targeting 2,000 by Dec 2025 (nextleap.app - Blinkit vs Zepto Product TearDown). Blinkit reportedly turned profitable in Q1 FY25 with a **₹43 crore profit** on **₹942 crore revenue** (nextleap.app - Blinkit vs Zepto Product TearDown), while Zepto’s losses persisted.

Both platforms face intense competition (base.com) and operate under similar gig economy dynamics, suggesting that the human capital pressures, low pay, and safety risks highlighted for the quick commerce sector broadly apply to both. The competition in a "Prisoner's Dilemma" scenario often leads to "defecting with discounts" or faster expansion, which can exacerbate "high burn, thin margins and more of capital dependence," ultimately placing greater pressure on unit economics and potentially, worker welfare (linkedin.com - arinverma).

### Risks & Social Outcomes

The quick commerce model, while offering consumer convenience, presents significant risks from a labor and human capital perspective:
*   **Unsustainable Pressure on Gig Workers:** The relentless pursuit of speed incentivizes risky behavior and can lead to low effective wages when factoring in operational costs and inconsistent demand.
*   **Regulatory Lag:** Current regulations struggle to keep pace with gig economy models, leaving workers vulnerable (grm.institute, livemint.com - 1175118505267).
*   **Ethical Concerns:** The "10-minute delivery" promise raises questions about the ethical limits of convenience and responsibility towards delivery partners (linkedin.com - arinverma).

In conclusion, while Zepto demonstrates impressive growth and strides towards operational efficiency, the sector's inherent challenges in unit economics, amplified by intense competition, frequently translate into considerable human capital pressure and social risks for gig workers. Sustainable profitability must eventually incorporate fair compensation and safe working conditions as core operational costs, rather than externalities.

*   **Average Order Value (AOV) & Waste:** Quick commerce largely thrives on "impulse purchases" and "urgent top-ups" (Livemint-11770014400091, base.blog). Zepto's AOV is typically ₹300-₹400 (GRM), aiming for ₹600-₹700 for profitability (base.blog). Blinkit's AOV was ₹625 in Q1FY25 (PPFAS).
    *   **Packaging Waste:** Frequent small orders contribute to a higher volume of **packaging waste** (IJRPR57035, IJRPR48607). Strategies like "bundled discounts and curated combos" (GRM) or bulk-buying features (Zepto's SuperSaver, Swiggy Instamart's Maxxsaver) aim to increase AOV (Livemint-11770014400091). While these could potentially reduce the *frequency* of individual deliveries and thus traffic/emissions, analysts warn that such growth is often "bought with deeper discounts, free-delivery thresholds, and incentives that weigh on margins," indicating a focus on volume over true efficiency gains (Livemint-11770014400091).
    *   **Spoilage:** Quick commerce dark stores, focusing on perishables, face a 5-15% spoilage rate (medium.com). Zepto has improved forecasting to "minimise wastage" (Livemint-11718950801678), a positive step towards **resource efficiency**.

**3. Growth vs. Burn: Pathways to Sustainable Profitability**

Zepto's operating revenue doubled to ₹4,454 crore in FY24, with losses remaining flat at ₹1,248 crore (Livemint-11734105378878). Zepto has successfully attracted significant investment, including a $665 million pre-IPO round at a $3.6 billion valuation (Livemint-11718939449690). The company claims 75% of its 350 dark stores are EBITDA-positive, reaching profitability in six months, down from 23 months previously (Livemint-11718950801678).

*   **Environmental Opportunities from Efficiency:** Zepto attributes its path to near PAT (net profit) positivity to "cost reduction through supply chain excellence" (Livemint-11709192210962). Improvements in "core productivity metrics" and reduced wastage directly correlate with enhanced **resource efficiency and reduced environmental footprint**. Investment in "low-emission fleets, optimised routing, and urban planning reforms can reduce the environmental strain" (Sarkar 2024 & Son and Kwon 2023, cited in IJRPR57035).
*   **Advertising Revenue:** A new, high-margin revenue stream, Zepto's ad revenue hit ₹400 crore and is projected to reach ₹1,000 crore (Livemint-11718950801678). Blinkit's ad revenue grew 220% YoY in Q3 FY24 (Livemint-11718950801678). This diversification could reduce the pressure for growth-at-any-cost, potentially allowing for greater investment in **green logistics practices** without solely relying on product margins (IJRPR57035, businessmodelcanvastemplate).

**4. Comparative Analysis (Zepto vs. Blinkit): Divergent Impacts**

Blinkit commands a larger market share (40-45% vs. Zepto's 28%) and a more extensive dark store network (Livemint-11718536515304, Facebook-Inc42, businessmodelcanvastemplate). Zepto's current model is inventory-led, while Blinkit and Instamart follow a marketplace model, though Zepto is transitioning to a marketplace structure (Livemint-11735963052646, Livemint-11734105378878).

*   **Carbon Emissions & Gridlock:** Both companies contribute to increased **carbon emissions and micro-fulfillment traffic gridlock** due to their reliance on frequent, fast deliveries in dense urban areas (IJRPR57035, IJRPR48607). The competitive landscape, likened to a "Prisoner's Dilemma" (LinkedIn-arinverma), often leads to "defect[ion] with discounts" and "faster expansion," resulting in "high burn, thin margins," which may discourage investment in costly but environmentally sound practices.

**Conclusion:**
While Zepto demonstrates strong operational efficiencies and a clear path to profitability for its dark stores, the quick commerce model, in general, presents substantial environmental and social sustainability challenges. The core promise of speed drives high fixed costs, intensive last-mile logistics, increased small-vehicle movements, packaging waste, and puts significant pressure on gig workers. Opportunities for improved sustainability lie in leveraging operational efficiencies for green logistics, adopting low-emission fleets, minimizing packaging, and advocating for urban planning reforms that account for the burgeoning quick commerce footprint. Future audits should closely monitor the actual impact of "supply chain excellence" and diversified revenue streams on these critical environmental and social metrics.

ABSTRACT
This study examines how leading quick-commerce firms in India structure and operate their service models. Using a qualitative case study approach, the analysis
focuses on three major platforms—Blinkit, Zepto, and Swiggy Instamart. The findings show that while all three firms rely on an inventory-led dark-store model,
they differ in their strategic orientation. Blinkit emphasises speed and dense network expansion, Zepto prioritises operational efficiency, and Instamart builds on
platform integration within Swiggy’s delivery ecosystem. The study also identifies common constraints across firms, including high operating costs and
dependence on metropolitan demand. The results contribute to understanding the varied pathways through which quick-commerce models are developing in India.
Keywords: Quick commerce, dark stores, last-mile delivery, online retail, Indian e-commerce, service models
Introduction
Quick commerce has become an important part of India’s online retail sector. It offers very fast delivery of everyday goods through dark stores and
technology-based fulfilment systems. This model has grown quickly in large cities where customers expect speed and convenience, and where demand
is high enough to support frequent orders. The rise of smartphones, digital payments, and app-based shopping has also helped this format expand.
Although quick commerce has received wide attention in business news and industry reports, there is limited academic work that explains how these
firms actually operate. We still do not fully understand how different companies design their delivery systems, manage dark stores, or build their
service models. This gap makes it difficult to compare firms or to see how their strategies differ.
This study aims to fill this gap by examining three leading quick-commerce firms in India: Blinkit, Zepto, and Swiggy Instamart. The study uses a case-
study approach based on reliable secondary sources. It looks at how each firm sets up its dark-store network, manages last-mile delivery, and positions
itself in the market. The purpose is to understand how these firms interpret the quick-commerce model and how their approaches differ in practice.
By comparing these three cases, the study provides a clearer picture of the different quick-commerce models operating in India. It also highlights the
common features that support this service model, such as micro-fulfilment and city-based demand, as well as the challenges that firms face. This
understanding can help researchers and practitioners see how quick-commerce platforms evolve and how their strategies shape their growth.
Objectives of the Study
1. To examine how leading quick-commerce firms in India structure their service models.
2. To analyse the strategic approaches adopted by Blinkit, Zepto, and Swiggy Instamart.
3. To compare the similarities and differences across the three firms.
4. To understand the key challenges faced by quick-commerce firms in India.

delivery speed shapes consumer engagement. Harter, Stich, and Spann (2024) analyse delivery time as a key driver of repurchase behaviour and find

Paris and show that these micro-fulfilment centres reshape transport flows, increase small-vehicle movements, and alter neighbourhood dynamics.
Their work highlights concerns related to congestion, noise, and land-use conflicts, suggesting that the rapid expansion of dark stores has broader social
and spatial consequences.
Consumer experience within quick commerce platforms is also a major theme. Kapoor, Sindwani, and Goel (2023) investigate the service experience of
quick-commerce users through a combined SEM and fsQCA approach. They report that convenience, delivery accuracy, and customer support strongly
affect user satisfaction. Their findings also show that satisfaction is shaped by combinations of factors, rather than by single predictors, indicating that
user experience is multidimensional.
Environmental sustainability is another area of concern. Sarkar (2024) evaluates whether quick-delivery models can be considered environmentally
responsible. The study shows that frequent micro-deliveries increase emissions, energy use, and packaging waste. The author concludes that quick-

Similarly, Son and Kwon (2023) analyse air-quality impacts linked to rapid delivery systems and propose spatial strategies to minimise pollution. They

location of the user. Traditional e -commerce was focused on next -day deliveries or within a week delivery. This is being challenged by qcommerce

drastically changed the way people shop for groceries, daily essentials, and even medicines. Even though the major points of competition among these
companies are delivery speed and convenience, customer loyalty is another factor which is very important for keeping a competitive advantage. Customers
often shift between platforms based on different deals, pricing differences, and service quality. This paper puts focus on how blinkit and zepto use different

Zepto: Start-up set up by 2 Mumbai teens valued at
₹4,300 crore, after latest funding | Company Business News View Market Dashboard Start-up set up by 2 Mumbai teens valued at
₹4,300 crore, after latest funding Bloomberg Published 21 Dec 2021, 12:00 PM IST File photo of Zepto founders Aadit Palicha and Kaivalya Vohra Zepto, an instant grocery delivery startup founded by two teenagers, has raised $100 million in a funding round led by Y Combinator, taking its valuation to $570 million within five months of starting services in India’s red-hot quick commerce segment. The startup’s newest funding comes 45 days after a previous capital raise of $60 million at $225 million valuation, Aadit Palicha, co-founder and chief executive officer, said in an interview. Besides Y Combinator’s Continuity Fund, the latest round also received investments from new and existing backers like Glade Brook Capital Partners, Nexus Venture Partners, Breyer Capital and Silicon Valley investor Lachy Groom, the startup said in a statement.  Zepto, named after a minuscule unit of time, was started by Palicha and his childhood friend Kaivalya Vohra, both 19, who quit the coveted computer science program at Stanford University to return to India and get started in quick commerce. The startup, which promises to deliver grocery and daily essentials in 10 minutes, started in Mumbai earlier this year and has since expanded to Bangalore, Delhi and four other cities.   Online grocery delivery is taking off in India, a $1 trillion retail market where grocery purchases account for the bulk of the retail spending. Zepto is competing against startups like SoftBank Group Corp.-backed Blinkit, Google-backed Dunzo and Naspers Ltd.-backed Swiggy, as well as the likes of Amazon.com Inc. and Walmart Inc.-backed Flipkart. Zepto is growing quickly and its core unit economics are strong, Palicha said in the statement. Delivering within 10 minutes is “game changing,” said co-founder Vohra, the chief technology officer. The new capital will allow the Mumbai-based startup to grow its team and expand to more cities.  The firm has 100 so-called dark stores, or micro-warehouses, within high-demand neighborhoods and uses technology to perform tasks such as deciding store locations, placement of products and mapping out delivery routes to avoid traffic congestion. It now delivers over 2,500 items including fresh produce, cooking essentials, snacks and beverages, personal care items and home cleaning products. The founders bring “Doordash-like” execution to the quick commerce model, said Anu Hariharan, a partner at Y Combinator’s Continuity Fund. The startup is adding 100,000 new customers every week, she said in the statement.   Catch all the

Zepto nears unicorn status with $200 mn fundraising | Company Business News View Market Dashboard Zepto nears unicorn status with $200 mn fundraising
Anuj Suvarna Updated 2 May 2022, 11:51 PM IST Zepto was founded by Aadit Palicha and Kaivalya Vohra, dropouts of computer science degree course at Stanford University. AI Quick Read Kiranakart Technologies Pvt. Ltd, which runs 10-minute grocery delivery platform Zepto, has raised $200 million in a Series D funding round led by American technology startup accelerator Y Combinator’s Continuity Fund, which valued it at $900 million.   The round also saw the participation of new investor Kaiser Permanente, besides existing investors Nexus Venture Partners, Glade Brook Capital and Lachy Groom. "Rather than optimising for a 10-15% higher valuation, we wanted to optimise for the right long-term partner, like Y Combinator. More importantly, we wanted to have enough cash in the bank where we are not affected by market sentiment cycles. it was not a tough round given Zepto’s focus on solid execution and reaching a high level of scale sustainably,” said Aadit Palicha, Zepto co-founder and chief executive. Zepto claims to have posted 800% quarter-on-quarter growth in revenue , while reducing its burn rate 5X on a per-order basis.“We maintained a phenomenal 88-point NPS (net promoter score) and 60% month-1 buyer retention at scale.” Palicha said it was easy to raise funds given its focus on execution achieving the highest scale of business among competitors. The fresh capital will be deployed for growing ‘sustainably’ across India, as it looks to hire across engineering, analytics, operations, marketing, finance, and human resource divisions.   Zepto has over 50,000 delivery partners and around 1,000 employees. It plans to double its employee base in the next one year.  “Our rigorous focus on unit economics is the main reason why we’ve had such an amazing trajectory as a company. We have turned micro-markets profitable and brought down burn significantly while growing to a scale of hundreds of thousands of orders per day,” said Kaivalya Vohra, co-founder and chief technology officer, Zepto. "By the end of the quarter, we look to turn at least a dozen micro-markets profitable. We are already operating profitable in a few mature micro markets and our burn per order has reduced significantly. We have also focused on Unit Economics driven growth and we’re now at a high-level of scale compared to other players in Q-Commerce.” In January, Zepto had raised $100 million (around
₹ 758 crore) in a Series C round led by Continuity Fund at a valuation of $570 million (around
₹ 4,275 crore). In November, it had raised $60 million. So far, the company has raised $360 million. Palicha said Zepto wants to continue its focus on a high degree of efficiency rather than valuation and may raise the next round in a year. Zepto is also piloting a service to deliver coffee, tea, and other café items within 10 minutes in select areas of Mumbai under Zepto Café. He said early customers responded positively to its service. Zepto plans to scale it across India if the pilot goes well, the company said.  Zepto was founded by Palicha and Vohra, 18-year-old dropouts of computer science degree course at Stanford University. The company operates a network of cloud stores or micro-warehouses to deliver groceries in under 10 minutes. It is present in all major Indian cities. Catch all the

TITLE: Quick Commerce will rival e-commerce majors Amazon, Flipkart in 2025: Zepto CEO
CLEANED BODY:
Quick Commerce will rival e-commerce majors Amazon, Flipkart in 2025: Zepto CEO View Market Dashboard Quick Commerce will rival e-commerce majors Amazon, Flipkart in 2025: Zepto CEO PTI Updated 1 Jan 2025, 02:48 PM IST Quick Commerce will rival e-commerce majors Amazon, Flipkart in 2025: Zepto CEO AI Quick Read
New Delhi, Jan 1 (PTI) The quick commerce industry will reach a stage in 2025 where it will be comparable to traditional e-commerce giants like Amazon and Flipkart, Zepto co-founder and CEO Aadit Palicha has asserted.
In a LinkedIn post on New Year's Eve, he said Zepto last year announced that 2024 will be the year people realise that quick commerce has the potential to create an Amazon/Flipkart level outcome in India and outlined three key forecasts for the new year.
"In 2025, Quick Commerce will actually start hitting a scale where it will become comparable to e-commerce," he wrote.
IPO-headed Zepto reported a 120 per cent increase in operating revenue to
₹ 4,454 crore in FY24, surpassing competitors like Swiggy's Instamart and Zomato's Blinkit.
Emphasising that success in quick commerce will hinge on "exceptional execution", Palicha said it will be challenging for every single player to deliver that level of execution.
"In 2025, the fundamentals of Quick Commerce will evolve dramatically. The customer value proposition will level up quickly. The unit economics and initiatives to build operating leverage will change. The capital markets environment for this industry, from private to public, will also look different compared to 2024 and 2023," he wrote, expressing optimism for an incredible 2025.
Sharing some New Year's Eve sale metrics, Palicha said, "Zepto is up 200 per cent compared to last year", and handling lakhs of orders per hour.
"India’s New Year celebrations are in full swing -- 3,345 ice cube orders per hour and counting," he wrote.
Zepto's peer Blinkit said it hit the highest-ever orders in a day on December 31.
Blinkit CEO Albinder Dhindsa took to micro-blogging site X to share that the company has hit the highest OPM (orders per minute), highest OPH (orders per hour), and highest total tips given to delivery partners in a day.
Blinkit also recorded the most grapes sold in a day on New Year's Eve as Indians embraced the viral tradition of eating 12 grapes at midnight. The tradition, which has become a trending topic on social media, is rooted in Spanish culture and symbolises one grape for each month of the year to bring good luck and prosperity.

News Zepto plans bigger dark stores to fulfill quick-commerce orders
Sowmya Ramasubramanian ,  Ranjani Raghavan 4
min read 17 Jun 2024, 06:00 AM IST Zepto founders Aadit Palicha and Kaivalya Vohra. Summary Zepto is said to have received fundraise commitments upwards of $1 billion indicating rising investor interest in the quick-commerce sector Gift this article Check your wealth This is a Mint Premium article gifted to you. Subscribe to enjoy similar stories. Subscribe now Quick-commerce company Zepto is looking to set up bigger dark stores so it can stock a larger assortment of products, according to two people aware of the company's plans. The development comes as the online grocery platform seeks to move big-time into non-grocery delivery, and as investors queue up to buy a stake in the four-year-old startup. Over the coming months, Zepto will look to set up one or two big storage facilities in major cities. These will serve as hubs as the company moves to deliver, within minutes, high-value items such as electronics, appliances, gifting items, and even luxury goods like gaming consoles, the two people said on condition of anonymity. To be sure, Zepto already offers some of these categories in a few cities. However, larger stores will allow the storage of more stock-keeping units, which wouldn’t be possible in smaller grocery-focused stores. The idea is also to replenish goods in the smaller dark stores that exist within a 2-km radius in most major cities as per demand, one of the persons quoted above said. Also Read:
Flipkart has FOMO? Zepto and Blinkit are changing the e-commerce giant “Zepto is slowly adopting Swiggy Mall’s and Blinkit’s model of maintaining specific stores for non-grocery items and a greater number of small stores for fast-moving goods,” this person said. “While Zepto is making strides in ramping up e-commerce sales, its focus will remain on quick commerce.” According to the second person cited earlier, Zepto had even considered creating a separate tab in the smartphone application for e-commerce orders, much like Swiggy’s Mall feature, but decided against it keeping in mind its commitment to 10-minute grocery orders. Zepto currently operates roughly 250 dark stores across major cities like Mumbai, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, and Delhi-NCR, among others. A dark store is a captive retail store used to fulfil a company's online orders, and is not accessible to the end consumer. Queries sent to Zepto remained unanswered till press time. The expansion of quick commerce Quick commerce firms are increasingly looking to sell non-grocery items as the large ticket sizes help boost order values and volume. Consumer brands, too, are benefiting from this strategy with quick commerce platforms becoming a key sales channel for many. Also Read:
The rise of quick commerce has left us with a bagful of questions Last month, Honasa Consumer, which owns the brand Mamaearth, among others, said during its investors’ call that sales through quick commerce are
outpacing
traditional e-commerce platforms.  Pankaj Makkar, managing director of venture investment firm Bertelsmann India Investments, reiterated the value of these platforms and called quick commerce an “ evolution ” of e-commerce, in a recent interview with  Mint . The financials of leading players show the speed of growth of the quick commerce business. Market leader Blinkit’s FY24 operating revenue stood at
₹ 2,301 crore, as per announcements made as part of parent
Zomato 's Q4FY24 results. In FY23, revenues reportedly stood at around
₹ 724 crore. While Zepto’s FY24 revenue is not available yet, its FY23 revenue grew 14 times to
₹ 2,024 crore, up from
₹ 142 crore a year ago, according to data sourced from the portal of the ministry of corporate affairs. However, losses widened 226% to
₹ 1,272 crore during the year, proving the challenges of running a high-burn business. According to a report by HSBC Global Research from April, Zepto has steadily increased its quick-commerce market share to 28% at the cost of Swiggy Instamart over the past two years, while market leader Blinkit has grown its share to 40%. Back on investors’ radar Founded in 2021 by Aadit Palicha and Kaivalya Vohra, Zepto shot to fame after it secured upwards of $150 million in funding from marquee investors such as Y Combinator and Nexus Venture Partners within months of inception. It attained unicorn status last year after bagging $200 million in Series E funding led by US private equity investor StepStone Group, joined by Goodwater Capital and existing investors such as Nexus Venture Partners and Glade Brook Capital. Also Read:
How India could become the world’s first quick-commerce success story Zepto is now gearing up to raise fresh funds ahead of a public-market listing potentially in 2026. The firm is said to have received commitments upwards of $1 billion for its pre-IPO round, according to a third person aware of the matter. “The round is continuously growing larger. Now it’s up to Aadit to decide how much to pick in order to not dilute too much,” this person said, also requesting anonymity. The rising interest indicates the comeback of optimism in the quick commerce space, which was once dismissed by the venture capital and private equity ecosystem owing to its cash-guzzling proposition. However, quick commerce firms have managed to fuel consumer demand as well as show better unit economics, making the space highly competitive in recent months.  For instance, improved performance has helped Blinkit—Zomato’s grocery delivery app—drive up its implied value to $13 billion in March 2024 compared to $2 billion a year ago, as per a report by Goldman Sachs. News Mint Explainer: Why quick commerce wants planned buys even as margins come under pressure
Sowmya Ramasubramanian 3
min read 2 Feb 2026, 04:35 PM IST Despite the lift in order values and early uptake, analysts say the economics of bulk buying remain unproven. ( Bloomberg ) Summary Mint explains why platforms are chasing bulk buys and what this trade-off means for quick commerce strategies, especially as competition heats up. Gift this article Check your wealth This is a Mint Premium article gifted to you. Subscribe to enjoy similar stories. Subscribe now BENGALURU: Quick commerce platforms are pushing shoppers to buy in bulk to lift order values, but analysts warn the discount-heavy tactic may deepen, not solve, their profitability challenge. Swiggy Instamart and Zepto are doubling down on bulk-purchase and planned-order features, betting that nudging customers to buy more per order will raise average order values (AOV) and improve unit economics. The push appears to be delivering headline gains. Swiggy Instamart’s Maxxsaver drove its AOV up to
₹ 746 in the December quarter, from
₹ 534 a year earlier, underscoring growing consumer appetite for discounted larger carts. But analysts caution that much of this growth is being bought with deeper discounts, free-delivery thresholds, and incentives that weigh on margins. Larger baskets also increase packing and last-mile costs, raising questions about whether higher AOVs are translating into healthier economics. Also Read
|
Quick commerce ad spend surges as brands chase higher conversions Mint
explains why platforms are chasing bulk buys and what this trade-off means for quick commerce strategies ahead, especially as competition intensifies. From impulse to planned purchases Quick commerce
was built on impulse buys and urgent top-ups. But as platforms chase profitability, they are increasingly trying to steer users toward planned weekly or monthly orders that promise better cost efficiency. Swiggy Instamart rolled out Maxxsaver in April last year, offering savings of up to
₹ 500 on orders above
₹ 999 across its range of more than 35,000 products, including non-grocery items such as apparel and kitchenware. Zepto launched SuperSaver in September 2024, testing the feature in Bengaluru, with discounted prices on orders above
₹ 1,000. Nudging users toward planned purchases helps platforms smooth demand and improve unit economics, according to Satish Meena, analyst at market research firm Datum Intelligence. “This can improve overheads such as fulfillment and reduce per-order costs, which can meaningfully improve unit economics compared to single-item orders,” Meena said. Other variants of the same strategy are also being rolled out across platforms. Zepto introduced slotted deliveries in select pockets of Bengaluru in December, enabling users to choose a delivery window instead of opting for delivery within minutes. Swiggy Instamart allows users to combine orders with a longer wait time, helping it minimize the cost of multiple deliveries. Tata Group’s
BigBasket
continues to offer scheduled deliveries, a proposition on which it built its foundation, although quick deliveries are now the focus. Also Read
|
CCPA fines Zepto for hidden fees and tricky online checkout designs Scheduled deliveries also boost non-grocery order growth, which is essential for sustaining high AOV, Meena noted. Non-grocery orders accounted for 32% of Instamart’s December-quarter sales, its investor presentation showed. Zepto’s Super Saver has the potential to bring 3x the AOV of regular deliveries, CEO Aadit Palicha had told
Mint
[...]
Mint’s
queries on SuperSaver’s traction or strategy. Why analysts are wary Despite higher AOVs and early traction, analysts argue that the economics of bulk buying are far from settled. “Larger baskets do help improve order values, but the challenge is that quick commerce platforms are often inducing that behaviour through discounts and delivery incentives. Subscribe now Bengaluru: On a chilly winter evening, last year, Sanjith G.S., a student of engineering in Bengaluru, realized he needed a hair dryer. A quick online search led to many hair dryer options on the two familiar e-tailing platforms, Amazon and Flipkart. But to his surprise, Blinkit, known for delivering groceries, also popped up. What caught his attention most was the price tag—on Blinkit, a particular brand of hair dryer, selling for
₹ 999, was a tad cheaper when compared to the other online stores. While speed wasn’t a top priority, the swift delivery didn’t hurt. “Within minutes, I had the hair dryer in hand, along with some savings,” said Sanjith. Blinkit, owned by Zomato, is a quick commerce company. So are Swiggy Instamart and Zepto. They mostly deliver goods to our homes super quick, within 15-20 minutes. These companies have set up dark stores or micro-fulfillment centres across many neighbourhoods in a city to facilitate the stocking, packing and fast dispatch of orders. Convenience-loving millennials in India’s large cities have lapped up their services—the gross merchandise value (GMV) of quick commerce, or the value of all goods sold, have jumped from a negligible $0.1 billion in 2020 to $2.8 billion in 2023, according to estimates by Redseer, an advisory firm. All quick commerce companies started with groceries, or by selling stuff such as potatoes, tomatoes, mangoes, apples, rice and oil. But more recently, like Sanjith discovered, they stock everything from hair dryers and induction cooktops to smart watches and dildos. This has become a headache for Flipkart, one of India’s largest e-tailing companies with revenue of
₹ 55,823 crore in 2022-23. Selling everything is its domain. That’s what the company, now owned by Walmart, pioneered in India for over 15 years. But this fortress is under threat. Elara Capital, an investment banking firm, in a report published in April this year, stated that quick commerce is impacting the sales of traditional and e-commerce companies in cities where they are operational. “3-6% of the monthly FMCG (fast-moving consumer goods)/grocery spends may move to quick commerce platforms led by convenience,” it stated. So, there’s an urgency within Flipkart to launch quick commerce to protect its turf. It could launch the service within weeks, some company employees told
Mint . None of them wanted to be identified. This would be the company’s third attempt. In 2015, Flipkart launched a quick service called Nearby to deliver goods within 60 minutes. It closed down as the unit economics did not make sense at that time. In 2020, the company tried the model again with a service called Flipkart Quick. It shut down for broadly the same reasons, the employees
Mint

But the quick commerce model, which many industry observers wrote off even two years back, appears to be working now. “Based on what we’ve estimated, in 2024-25, quick commerce should be able to register explosive growth, 70-80% or in that range,” Kushal Bhatnagar, associate partner at Redseer, said. According to the people
Mint
spoke to, Flipkart is currently gripped by the fear of missing out (FOMO). What can it do to capitalize on the quick commerce wave? Flipkart didn’t respond to clarifications sought by
Mint . Early but not quick Before delving into Flipkart’s strategy, let’s first understand what went wrong with its previous two endeavours. Flipkart Nearby, launched in 2015, was a 60 minutes delivery service. Flipkart Quick in 2020 promised delivery in 90 minutes. Both the experiments were largely around delivery of food, vegetables and other grocery items. The 2015 venture was a five-month pilot in Bengaluru. Flipkart was way ahead of its time—the term ‘quick commerce’ was not in vogue back then and the idea did not vibe with consumers, former employees said. The company was using its existing supply chain infrastructure for logistics, the person added. “The legacy supply chain was not designed for speed. Zepto has a weekly replenishment plan, which helps the company maintain higher in-stock numbers and better availability for customers. Samiksha Goel 3
min read 2 Apr 2026, 03:30 PM IST Swiggy, Zomato and Zepto have scaled down their 10-minute food and snack delivery bets. (image: Pexel) Summary Hara Global and Bain Capital-backed startup Swish has raised about $38 million, nearly doubling its valuation. So what is it doing differently to attract investor interest? Can the 10-minute food delivery finally crack unit economics? Gift this article Check your wealth This is a Mint Premium article gifted to you. Subscribe to enjoy similar stories. Subscribe now Food delivery majors Swiggy and Zomato have both scaled back their bets on 10-minute food delivery after struggling to make the economics work. The model promised speed, but struggled to deliver profits. Now, a new startup, Swish, is attempting to do things differently with a focused, full-stack model that allows more flexibility and better margins. The 10-minute snack and fresh food delivery platform, recently raised $38 million in a Series B round led by Hara Global and Bain Capital Ventures, with participation from Accel, Stride Ventures, and Alteria Capital, bringing its total funding to about $54 million to date. So what is Swish doing differently to attract investor interest? Will the 10-minute delivery finally crack unit economics?
Mint
unpacks What is the story so far? Zomato
has made three attempts at ultra-fast food delivery: Instant, Everyday, and Quick. Each was built on a different model but ended for the same reason: the economics never worked. Instant’s pre-prepped 10-minute menu shut down within months, Everyday’s home-chef service faded out, and Quick’s 15-minute experiment was wound down after just four months. Zomato, in its most recent quick experiment, has since pivoted to Bistro, a more controlled, high-frequency menu run through dedicated kitchens.
Zepto
consolidated its Zepto Café offering last year, and in February, Swiggy shut Snacc, citing unsustainable costs. Swish recently raised about $38 million, nearly doubling its valuation, raising questions about what it’s doing differently and whether 10-minute food delivery can finally crack unit economics.
Also Read
|
Swiggy’s Snacc shutdown lays bare brutal economics of ultra-fast food delivery Why exactly are incumbents scaling down? It’s a complex model. Incumbents like Swiggy, Zomato and even Zepto have scaled down their
10-minute food
and snack delivery bets because the underlying economics never settled into a predictable, profitable pattern. The model depends on dense order volumes, hyper-local assortment, low rider idle time and high repeat behaviour, conditions that proved inconsistent outside a few micro-pockets. Delivery costs remained high, while average order values stayed low, creating a persistent gap between contribution margin and the cost of running dark stores or maintaining ultra-fast fleets. Over time, the marketplace companies seem to have shifted focus to higher-certainty bets, such as core food delivery, or 10-minute grocery, where frequency, margins, and operational leverage are more reliable. What are the other concerns with 10-minute delivery? Concerns around ultra-fast delivery go beyond business viability. In January, the Indian government urged quick-commerce companies to drop “10-minute delivery” claims, citing rising safety risks for gig workers. The move followed a nationwide strike in December, when thousands of delivery riders protested low pay, constant pressure, and dangerous road conditions. This didn’t massively disrupt customers, but it did ignite a wider debate around whether the speed customers enjoy comes at too high a human cost. After meeting with platform officials, the labour ministry urged companies to avoid tight delivery deadlines that could encourage risky behaviour on the road. The scrutiny has added a new layer of regulatory and ethical pressure on the 10-minute model.
Also Read
|
10-minute delivery backlash forces a rethink: execution over speed What is Swish doing differently? Swish’s pitch is that speed only works when the entire chain is tightly controlled. Instead of relying on restaurant partners or dark-store add-ons, the company runs its own compact, purpose-built cloud kitchens. Every step from cooking, garnishing, packaging, to delivery routing is handled in-house. This lets the team prepare and pack an order in about six to seven minutes and deliver it within a small 1.5–2 km radius, keeping food hot and quality consistent. Swish also keeps costs down by maintaining low capex and avoiding marketplace commissions altogether. The company argues that a focused, full-stack model allows more flexibility and better margins, something larger platforms struggle with because their kitchens are extensions of broader operations rather than standalone systems.
Also Read
|
Rapido’s new order: disrupting India’s food apps with lower fees What does investor behavior signal now? Investor interest suggests that the quick-food model isn’t dead, just waiting for the right execution. India’s food delivery market is projected to reach $15 billion by 2027, and ultra-fast formats are expected to take a growing slice of that demand. Swish’s recent $38 million Series B round indicates that backers believe the company has found early product–market fit and is scaling with discipline. Investors point to its operational efficiency, repeat usage, and tight cost controls as signs that this version of the 10-minute model may finally be workable. Unit economics are still unproven, but the funding signals a renewed willingness to bet on quick delivery, provided the model is controlled, capital-efficient, and data-driven. Zepto rolls out subscription service, fuels competition in quick commerce market | Company Business News View Market Dashboard Zepto rolls out subscription service, fuels competition in quick commerce market
Suneera Tandon Published 29 Feb 2024, 02:48 PM IST Zepto was founded by Aadit Palicha and Kaivalya Vohra, dropouts of computer science degree course at Stanford University. New Delhi: Quick commerce firm Zepto on Thursday officially announced the launch of its paid subscription service—Zepto Pass—that will offer greater discounts to consumers, ratcheting up competition in India’s growing quick commerce market. “The company has piloted Zepto Pass with 5% of its user base for a month and seen rapid adoption—almost a majority of orders came from Zepto Pass subscribers within two weeks during the pilot," it said in a statement. According to the company, those who subscribed to the Zepto Pass increased their spending on the app by over 30% and showed a 10% increase in monthly retention. The subscription, priced between 19 and 39 rupees per month, for a majority of customers, offers unlimited free deliveries and up to 20% off on grocery items. The move is intended to drive customer adoption of quick commerce, said Devendra Meel, vice president, Strategy, and head of Zepto Pass. Founded in 2021 by Stanford University dropouts Aadit Palicha and Kaivalya Vohra, Zepto promises 10-minute grocery deliveries through its network of dark stores nationwide. It competes in the highly competitive quick commerce arena, alongside players like Swiggy's Instamart, BigBasket's BBNow, Zomato's Blinkit, and Google-backed Dunzo, all vying for market dominance and profitability. Zepto hopes to draw a million customers into the subscription programme within a month of the rollout.  “Even after offering compelling value to customers with Zepto Pass, we continue to be on track to be near PAT (net profit) positive in two quarters. This is because our profitability improvement is coming primarily from cost reduction through supply chain excellence, which gives us the ability to pass value back to customers sustainably,” said Palicha, co-founder and CEO, Zepto. Palicha also highlighted that the subscription model is expected to raise the average order value (AOV), offsetting the cost of discounts through a mix of company and brand funding.  “We're in this position where we can afford to take the hit, because our supply chain costs have come down dramatically. All of our core productivity metrics have improved, throughput has improved and our wastage has reduced,” Palicha said in an interview. Zepto's move comes as quick commerce firms scramble to meet customer demands, expand market presence, and diversify their product offerings. Estimates by consulting firm RedSeer suggest that India’s quick commerce market is expected to touch $5.5 billion by 2025, up from $0.3 billion in 2021. Meanwhile, Palicha said the company is on track for a public listing in fiscal year 2025-26 (FY26).  “Obviously, it depends on the cost of capital, depends on where we see private markets going but right now we are building out all the internal infrastructure—whether it's the financial controls, whether it's the reporting, whether it's having a big board or a statutory auditor, all the core controls needed, we are building out to be a public company,” he said.
